rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is `explicit_self`, indicating the item is explicitly within scope. The verbatim text proposes 'Right to Repair' legislation that requires manufacturers to provide repair information 'to consumers and third-party technicians about how to repair damaged products.' This is a direct Resource Efficiency intervention (repair/reuse, product lifetime extension), aligning with the provided canonical mechanism 'right_to_repair_legislation → embodied_emissions_reduction_via_product_lifetime_extension.' The gate failure is due to missing outcome tokens, but the action described is unambiguously resource-efficiency related, so the row should be kept.
policy_statement: Enact legislation supporting 'Right to Repair' by requiring manufacturers to provide repair information to consumers and third-party technicians.
mechanism_description: The State should enact legislation that requires manufacturers to provide repair information, enabling more repairs and longer product lifetimes that reduce emissions associated with producing replacement goods.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (functional products) → ↓ demand for new product manufacturing → ↓ material/industrial energy use → ↓ embodied GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Manufacturers must provide parts, tools, documentation, and/or diagnostic access so consumers and independent repair providers can repair products rather than replace them.  
(2) Mediating physical/industrial process: Increased repair and reuse extends product lifetimes, reducing throughput of raw material extraction, component fabrication, assembly, and global supply-chain transport required for new units.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower lifecycle (embodied) GHG emissions from avoided production of replacement goods (and often some reduction in waste management emissions as a co-benefit).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided production of new goods (and associated embodied emissions) because products are repaired and kept in use longer.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Extending product lifetime increases service delivered per unit of material, thereby reducing demand for new manufacturing and its embodied emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the outcome is GHG reduction (which relates to Mitigation), the defining mechanism is reduced material and product turnover—i.e., efficiency in resource use—so the most general primary category across all such policies is Resource Efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some right-to-repair policies may also reduce operational energy (e.g., keeping older, less efficient appliances longer could slightly increase use-phase emissions) or affect e-waste; these do not typically outweigh the mechanism’s central intent and effect of avoided new production, though they can matter by product class.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that improves repair access/information to extend lifetimes operates through reduced replacement purchasing and avoided production, fitting Resource Efficiency consistently.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Any further sub-classification (e.g., electronics vs appliances vs vehicles; net lifecycle benefit magnitude) depends on policy scope and product mix and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG impact can vary by product category and energy intensity in the use phase (e.g., extending life of inefficient refrigerators or vehicles could increase operational emissions); pairing with efficiency standards or targeted scope can mitigate this, but the dominant mechanism remains avoided embodied emissions from reduced replacement demand.
key_indicators: "enact legislation"; "requires manufacturers"; "provide information"; "consumers and third-party technicians"; "how to repair damaged products"; "Right to Repair"
co_benefits: Reduced solid waste/e-waste generation; consumer cost savings; local repair job support; reduced supply-chain impacts beyond GHGs
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a regulatory mandate (compelling manufacturer behavior), but the operative requirement in the text is specifically *providing repair information*, which fits best as an information-disclosure instrument. If the statute also mandated parts/tools/diagnostic access, it would shift more clearly toward regulatory-mandate.
